Problem statement
Design an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache with O(1) get and put, automatic eviction when full, and thread-safety for concurrent access. This is the canonical data-structure-heavy LLD.

Core Requirements:

Get: return the value for a key in O(1) and mark it most-recently-used; miss is handled explicitly.
Put: insert/update a key in O(1); when over capacity, evict the least-recently-used entry.
Remove: delete a key.
Eviction Policy: which entry to evict should be pluggable (LRU default; LFU as a harder cousin).
Validation: reject a non-positive capacity.

Additional Considerations:

O(1) for both get and put (HashMap + doubly-linked list).
Thread-safety under heavy concurrent get/put; prefer lock striping / segmentation over one global lock.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. LRUCache, Node, DoublyLinkedList, EvictionPolicy).
Use a HashMap (key -> node) plus a doubly-linked list (MRU front, LRU tail) for O(1) reorder/evict.
Discuss design patterns (Strategy for eviction, Template Method for the lock hook).
Focus on the O(1) data structure and on lock striping for concurrency.

# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Thread-Safe LRU Cache.

The whole design rests on ONE structural idea: a HashMap gives O(1) lookup by
key, and a doubly-linked list gives O(1) reordering by recency. Glue them
together and BOTH `get` and `put` are O(1) — the list remembers "who was used
when", so eviction is just "drop the tail".

This file holds the two raw building blocks (`Node`, `DoublyLinkedList`) and the
typed exceptions. The recency/eviction *policy* lives in `strategies.py`; the
orchestration plus thread-safety live in `cache_service.py`.

🎙️ "Two data structures, one trick: the map answers 'where is this key', the
linked list answers 'who is the coldest entry'. Neither alone gives O(1) for both
get and put — together they do."
"""

from typing import Generic, Iterator, TypeVar

K = TypeVar("K")
V = TypeVar("V")


# ── EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) ─────────────────────
class CacheError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this cache raises — callers catch ONE type."""


class KeyNotFoundError(CacheError):
    """get()/remove() asked for a key that isn't present — a miss made explicit."""


class InvalidCapacityError(CacheError):
    """Capacity (or segment count) was below 1 — a cache with no room is meaningless."""


# ── NODE: one entry, living in BOTH the map and the recency list ─────────────
class Node(Generic[K, V]):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # One cache entry. It sits simultaneously in the HashMap (found by key) and in
    # the doubly-linked list (found by position). It carries `key` as well as
    # `value` so that when we evict the tail node we know which MAP entry to delete.
    # 🎙️ "The node stores its own key. Without that back-pointer, evicting the tail
    # would mean a scan to find which key owned it — O(n). With it, eviction is O(1)."
    __slots__ = ("key", "value", "prev", "next")

    def __init__(self, key: K, value: V) -> None:
        self.key = key
        self.value = value
        # WHY: every real node ends up strictly between two sentinels, so prev/next
        #      are never None in practice — the | None is only for construction.
        self.prev: "Node[K, V] | None" = None
        self.next: "Node[K, V] | None" = None

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        return f"Node({self.key!r}={self.value!r})"


# ── DOUBLY-LINKED LIST: the recency order (MRU at front, LRU at back) ─────────
class DoublyLinkedList(Generic[K, V]):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The recency order. Front (right after head) = most-recently-used; back (right
    # before tail) = least-recently-used. Two SENTINEL nodes (head, tail) that never
    # hold data mean every real node always has a non-null prev and next — so insert,
    # remove, and move-to-front are branch-free O(1) with no "is this the first node?"
    # edge case to get wrong.
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        # Sentinels: head <-> tail when empty. Real nodes go strictly between them.
        self._head: Node[K, V] = Node(None, None)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
        self._tail: Node[K, V] = Node(None, None)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
        self._head.next = self._tail
        self._tail.prev = self._head

    def push_front(self, node: Node[K, V]) -> None:
        """Insert `node` as the new most-recently-used (right after head). O(1)."""
        first = self._head.next
        node.prev = self._head
        node.next = first
        self._head.next = node
        first.prev = node  # type: ignore[union-attr]

    def remove(self, node: Node[K, V]) -> None:
        """Unlink `node` from wherever it sits. O(1) — we already hold both neighbours."""
        # WHY: because of the sentinels, node.prev and node.next are ALWAYS real
        #      nodes, so there is no head/tail special-case here.
        node.prev.next = node.next  # type: ignore[union-attr]
        node.next.prev = node.prev  # type: ignore[union-attr]
        node.prev = None
        node.next = None

    def move_to_front(self, node: Node[K, V]) -> None:
        """Mark `node` most-recently-used: unlink it, then re-insert at the front. O(1)."""
        self.remove(node)
        self.push_front(node)

    def back(self) -> Node[K, V]:
        """Peek the least-recently-used node WITHOUT removing it (the victim candidate). O(1)."""
        lru = self._tail.prev
        if lru is self._head:
            raise CacheError("back() called on an empty recency list")
        return lru  # type: ignore[return-value]

    def is_empty(self) -> bool:
        return self._head.next is self._tail

    def iter_back_to_front(self) -> Iterator[Node[K, V]]:
        """Walk nodes from least- to most-recently-used. LFU uses this to break ties by recency."""
        node = self._tail.prev
        while node is not self._head:
            yield node  # type: ignore[misc]
            node = node.prev  # type: ignore[assignment]

# Python 3.11+
"""LRUCache — the clean, single-threaded core — plus two thread-safe variants.

Layering (each layer adds exactly ONE idea on top of the last):

  1. LRUCache             HashMap + doubly-linked list. O(1) get/put. NO locks.
                          A no-op `_lock(key)` hook MARKS every critical section.
  2. SynchronizedLRUCache overrides `_lock` to take ONE global RLock. Correct, but
                          every operation serializes. The simple-but-serial baseline.
  3. SegmentedLRUCache    lock striping: S independent sub-caches, each its own
                          map + list + lock. A key hashes to one segment, so threads
                          touching different keys never wait on each other. This is
                          the Java ConcurrentHashMap / Guava model — the throughput win.

🎙️ "The core has zero lock code — locks would only muddy the data-structure story.
Thread-safety is a second, deliberate layer: one global lock for correctness, then
lock striping for throughput. The clean core never changes between the two."
"""

import threading
from contextlib import contextmanager
from typing import Callable, Generic, Iterator, TypeVar

from models import (
    DoublyLinkedList,
    InvalidCapacityError,
    KeyNotFoundError,
    Node,
)
from strategies import EvictionPolicy, LRUEviction

K = TypeVar("K")
V = TypeVar("V")


class LRUCache(Generic[K, V]):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The brain. It owns the HashMap (key -> Node) for O(1) lookup and the
    # doubly-linked list for O(1) recency reorder. Together they make BOTH get and
    # put O(1): the map finds the node, the list moves it to the front, eviction
    # pops the tail. Victim selection is delegated to a pluggable EvictionPolicy.
    def __init__(self, capacity: int, policy: EvictionPolicy | None = None) -> None:
        if capacity < 1:
            raise InvalidCapacityError(f"capacity must be >= 1, got {capacity}")
        self._capacity = capacity
        self._map: dict[K, Node[K, V]] = {}
        self._order: DoublyLinkedList[K, V] = DoublyLinkedList()
        self._policy = policy or LRUEviction()

    # ── Template-Method hook: no-op here; subclasses drop in a real lock ──────
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, key: K) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean core — there is zero lock code in this
        #      class. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this one method, so the
        #      diff between "clean" and "locked" is this hook, never the business logic.
        yield

    # ── get: O(1) lookup, then mark most-recently-used ────────────────────────
    def get(self, key: K) -> V:
        with self._lock(key):
            node = self._map.get(key)
            if node is None:
                raise KeyNotFoundError(f"key not found: {key!r}")
            self._order.move_to_front(node)  # this access is now the most recent
            self._policy.record_access(node)
            return node.value

    def get_or_none(self, key: K) -> V | None:
        # WHY: same as get(), but a miss is a normal answer (None), not an error.
        #      A hit still counts as a real access, so we reorder and record it.
        with self._lock(key):
            node = self._map.get(key)
            if node is None:
                return None
            self._order.move_to_front(node)
            self._policy.record_access(node)
            return node.value

    # ── put: insert or update, evicting the policy's victim when over capacity ──
    def put(self, key: K, value: V) -> None:
        with self._lock(key):
            existing = self._map.get(key)
            if existing is not None:
                existing.value = value               # update in place...
                self._order.move_to_front(existing)  # ...and it is now most-recent
                self._policy.record_access(existing)
                return
            node: Node[K, V] = Node(key, value)
            self._map[key] = node
            self._order.push_front(node)
            self._policy.record_insert(node)
            if len(self._map) > self._capacity:
                self._evict_one()

    # ── remove: explicit delete of a key ──────────────────────────────────────
    def remove(self, key: K) -> None:
        with self._lock(key):
            node = self._map.pop(key, None)
            if node is None:
                raise KeyNotFoundError(f"key not found: {key!r}")
            self._order.remove(node)
            self._policy.record_removal(node)

    # ── private helper: drop exactly one victim chosen by the policy ──────────
    def _evict_one(self) -> None:
        # WHY: the cache decides WHEN to evict (over capacity); the policy decides
        #      WHO. We unlink the victim from both structures and tell the policy,
        #      so no structure is left holding a dangling reference.
        victim = self._policy.select_victim(self._order)
        self._order.remove(victim)
        del self._map[victim.key]
        self._policy.record_removal(victim)

    # ── size / capacity introspection ─────────────────────────────────────────
    def __len__(self) -> int:
        with self._lock(_SIZE_KEY):
            return len(self._map)

    @property
    def capacity(self) -> int:
        return self._capacity

    def is_full(self) -> bool:
        with self._lock(_SIZE_KEY):
            return len(self._map) >= self._capacity

    def __contains__(self, key: K) -> bool:
        # WHY: membership only — NO reorder, so a `in` check doesn't count as a use.
        with self._lock(key):
            return key in self._map


# Sentinel "key" for whole-cache reads (len/is_full). It routes to the global lock
# in the synchronized cache; in the plain core the lock is a no-op so it is ignored.
_SIZE_KEY = object()


class SynchronizedLRUCache(LRUCache[K, V]):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The simplest correct thread-safe version: ONE lock around everything. The
    # Template-Method hook means we change exactly one method (`_lock`) and reuse the
    # entire core untouched. Correct, but serial — only one thread is ever inside
    # get/put even for unrelated keys. That is the baseline SegmentedLRUCache beats.
    def __init__(self, capacity: int, policy: EvictionPolicy | None = None) -> None:
        super().__init__(capacity, policy)
        self._global_lock = threading.RLock()

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, key: K) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: ignore the key — ONE lock guards the whole cache. RLock (reentrant) so
        #      the SAME thread can re-acquire it if one guarded method ever calls
        #      another; a plain Lock would self-deadlock on that second acquire.
        with self._global_lock:
            yield


class SegmentedLRUCache(Generic[K, V]):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Lock striping. Instead of one lock for the whole cache, we split the keyspace
    # into S independent SEGMENTS — each a complete little LRU cache with its OWN
    # map, list, and lock. A key always hashes to the same segment, so two threads
    # working on keys in different segments never block each other. This is exactly
    # how Java's ConcurrentHashMap and Guava's cache scale concurrent writes.
    def __init__(
        self,
        capacity: int,
        segments: int = 16,
        policy_factory: Callable[[], EvictionPolicy] | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        if capacity < 1:
            raise InvalidCapacityError(f"capacity must be >= 1, got {capacity}")
        if segments < 1:
            raise InvalidCapacityError(f"segments must be >= 1, got {segments}")
        # WHY: never more segments than capacity, or a segment would round down to
        #      zero room and silently drop everything routed to it.
        segments = min(segments, capacity)
        make_policy: Callable[[], EvictionPolicy] = policy_factory or LRUEviction
        self._capacity = capacity
        # Split capacity so the per-segment caps SUM to exactly `capacity` — that is
        # what keeps the global invariant len(cache) <= capacity exact, not approximate.
        base, remainder = divmod(capacity, segments)
        self._segments: list[SynchronizedLRUCache[K, V]] = [
            SynchronizedLRUCache(base + (1 if i < remainder else 0), make_policy())
            for i in range(segments)
        ]

    def _segment_for(self, key: K) -> SynchronizedLRUCache[K, V]:
        # WHY: hash(key) mod S picks the segment. Same key -> same segment every
        #      time, so ALL of a key's operations land on one lock. abs() guards
        #      Python's negative hashes.
        return self._segments[abs(hash(key)) % len(self._segments)]

    def get(self, key: K) -> V:
        return self._segment_for(key).get(key)

    def get_or_none(self, key: K) -> V | None:
        return self._segment_for(key).get_or_none(key)

    def put(self, key: K, value: V) -> None:
        self._segment_for(key).put(key, value)

    def remove(self, key: K) -> None:
        self._segment_for(key).remove(key)

    def __contains__(self, key: K) -> bool:
        return key in self._segment_for(key)

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        # WHY: sum each segment's size; each __len__ takes that segment's OWN lock,
        #      so this is a consistent per-segment snapshot — never a torn read.
        return sum(len(seg) for seg in self._segments)

    @property
    def capacity(self) -> int:
        return self._capacity

    def is_full(self) -> bool:
        return len(self) >= self._capacity

    @property
    def segment_count(self) -> int:
        return len(self._segments)

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Thread-Safe LRU Cache.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK".
"""

import threading

from cache_service import LRUCache, SegmentedLRUCache
from models import InvalidCapacityError, KeyNotFoundError
from strategies import LFUEviction


def main() -> None:
    # ── Case 1: get/put are O(1) and a hit returns the stored value ──
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    print(f"Case 1: put a=1, b=2 -> get('a') = {cache.get('a')}, len = {len(cache)}")
    assert cache.get("a") == 1
    assert len(cache) == 2
    print("Case 1: a stored entry reads back in O(1) via the HashMap -> OK")

    # ── Case 2: eviction drops the LEAST-recently-used entry ──
    cache = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    cache.get("a")            # touch 'a' -> now 'b' is the least-recently-used
    cache.put("c", 3)         # over capacity -> evict 'b' (the LRU), not 'a'
    print(f"Case 2: after touch('a') + put('c'): 'a' in? {'a' in cache}, 'b' in? {'b' in cache}")
    assert "a" in cache and "c" in cache
    assert "b" not in cache
    assert len(cache) == 2
    print("Case 2: access reorders recency; the coldest entry is evicted -> OK")

    # ── Case 3: updating an existing key does NOT grow the cache ──
    cache = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("x", 1)
    cache.put("x", 99)        # same key -> overwrite in place, size stays 1
    print(f"Case 3: put('x',1) then put('x',99) -> get('x') = {cache.get('x')}, len = {len(cache)}")
    assert cache.get("x") == 99
    assert len(cache) == 1
    print("Case 3: re-putting a key updates the value, size unchanged -> OK")

    # ── Case 4: typed errors on the miss path and on a bad capacity ──
    cache = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    try:
        cache.get("missing")
        assert False, "should have raised"
    except KeyNotFoundError as e:
        print(f"Case 4a: get('missing') -> {type(e).__name__}")
    try:
        LRUCache(capacity=0)
        assert False, "should have raised"
    except InvalidCapacityError as e:
        print(f"Case 4b: LRUCache(0) -> {type(e).__name__}")
    print("Case 4: misses and bad capacity raise typed CacheError subclasses -> OK")

    # ── Case 5: a pluggable LFU policy evicts the least-FREQUENTLY-used entry ──
    lfu: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2, policy=LFUEviction())
    lfu.put("a", 1)
    lfu.put("b", 2)
    lfu.get("a")              # freq: a=2, b=1
    lfu.get("a")              # freq: a=3, b=1
    lfu.put("c", 3)           # over capacity -> LFU evicts 'b' (used least)
    print(f"Case 5: LFU after a is hot, b cold, put('c'): 'a' in? {'a' in lfu}, 'b' in? {'b' in lfu}")
    assert "a" in lfu and "c" in lfu
    assert "b" not in lfu
    print("Case 5: swapping the eviction Strategy to LFU needs ZERO cache changes -> OK")

    # ── Case 6: lock striping survives a concurrent stampede with no corruption ──
    capacity = 20_000
    seg_cache: SegmentedLRUCache[str, str] = SegmentedLRUCache(capacity=capacity, segments=16)
    n_threads = 16
    per_thread = 800
    start = threading.Barrier(n_threads)

    def writer(t: int) -> None:
        start.wait()                       # all threads hit the cache at one instant
        for i in range(per_thread):
            key = f"t{t}-k{i}"
            seg_cache.put(key, f"{key}-val")
            assert seg_cache.get(key) == f"{key}-val"

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=writer, args=(t,)) for t in range(n_threads)]
    for th in threads:
        th.start()
    for th in threads:
        th.join()

    total = n_threads * per_thread
    print(f"Case 6: {n_threads} threads x {per_thread} keys, len = {len(seg_cache)} (<= {capacity})")
    assert len(seg_cache) == total, len(seg_cache)
    assert len(seg_cache) <= capacity
    for t in range(n_threads):
        for i in range(per_thread):
            key = f"t{t}-k{i}"
            assert seg_cache.get(key) == f"{key}-val"
    print("Case 6: striped locks -> no lost or torn nodes under heavy concurrency -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Thread-Safe LRU Cache.

Covers: the doubly-linked list primitive, every public cache method, the typed
error paths, the LRU eviction ORDER (access reorders; the coldest entry is
evicted), update-in-place not growing the cache, both eviction policies, and a
STRICT threading.Barrier concurrency proof on the segmented (lock-striped) cache.
"""

import threading

import pytest

from cache_service import LRUCache, SegmentedLRUCache, SynchronizedLRUCache
from models import (
    DoublyLinkedList,
    InvalidCapacityError,
    KeyNotFoundError,
    Node,
)
from strategies import LFUEviction, LRUEviction


# ── DoublyLinkedList primitive (unit) ──
def test_dll_push_front_and_back_order():
    dll: DoublyLinkedList[str, int] = DoublyLinkedList()
    a, b, c = Node("a", 1), Node("b", 2), Node("c", 3)
    dll.push_front(a)
    dll.push_front(b)
    dll.push_front(c)              # order front->back: c, b, a
    assert dll.back() is a         # 'a' was pushed first -> it is the LRU end
    assert not dll.is_empty()


def test_dll_move_to_front_changes_lru():
    dll: DoublyLinkedList[str, int] = DoublyLinkedList()
    a, b = Node("a", 1), Node("b", 2)
    dll.push_front(a)
    dll.push_front(b)              # front->back: b, a  => back is 'a'
    dll.move_to_front(a)           # front->back: a, b  => back is now 'b'
    assert dll.back() is b


def test_dll_remove_unlinks_node():
    dll: DoublyLinkedList[str, int] = DoublyLinkedList()
    a, b = Node("a", 1), Node("b", 2)
    dll.push_front(a)
    dll.push_front(b)
    dll.remove(b)
    assert dll.back() is a
    dll.remove(a)
    assert dll.is_empty()


# ── happy paths, one per public method ──
def test_put_then_get_returns_value():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    assert cache.get("a") == 1


def test_get_or_none_hit_and_miss():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    assert cache.get_or_none("a") == 1
    assert cache.get_or_none("nope") is None     # miss is a normal None, not an error


def test_remove_deletes_key():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.remove("a")
    assert "a" not in cache
    assert len(cache) == 0


def test_len_and_is_full_and_capacity():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    assert cache.capacity == 2
    assert not cache.is_full()
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    assert cache.is_full()
    assert len(cache) == 2


def test_value_none_is_stored_distinctly_from_missing():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int | None] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", None)
    assert "a" in cache
    assert cache.get_or_none("a") is None        # present-with-None, not absent
    assert cache.get("a") is None


# ── typed error paths ──
def test_get_missing_raises_key_not_found():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    with pytest.raises(KeyNotFoundError):
        cache.get("missing")


def test_remove_missing_raises_key_not_found():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    with pytest.raises(KeyNotFoundError):
        cache.remove("missing")


@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad", [0, -1, -5])
def test_non_positive_capacity_rejected(bad):
    with pytest.raises(InvalidCapacityError):
        LRUCache(capacity=bad)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_segments", [0, -3])
def test_segmented_rejects_bad_segment_count(bad_segments):
    with pytest.raises(InvalidCapacityError):
        SegmentedLRUCache(capacity=8, segments=bad_segments)


# ── eviction ORDER: access reorders recency; the LRU entry is evicted ──
def test_lru_evicts_least_recently_used():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    cache.get("a")                 # 'a' is now most-recent -> 'b' is the LRU
    cache.put("c", 3)              # over capacity -> evict 'b'
    assert "a" in cache and "c" in cache
    assert "b" not in cache
    assert len(cache) == 2


def test_lru_eviction_without_access_drops_oldest():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    cache.put("c", 3)              # no access between -> 'a' (oldest) is evicted
    assert "a" not in cache
    assert "b" in cache and "c" in cache


# ── update-in-place must NOT grow the cache ──
def test_update_existing_key_does_not_grow_size():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("x", 1)
    cache.put("x", 2)
    cache.put("x", 3)
    assert len(cache) == 1
    assert cache.get("x") == 3


def test_update_existing_key_refreshes_recency():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    cache.put("a", 11)             # re-put 'a' -> 'a' becomes most-recent, 'b' the LRU
    cache.put("c", 3)              # evict 'b'
    assert "a" in cache and "c" in cache
    assert "b" not in cache
    assert cache.get("a") == 11


# ── both eviction policies via the same cache, swapped by Strategy ──
def test_lfu_evicts_least_frequently_used():
    cache: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2, policy=LFUEviction())
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    cache.get("a")                 # freq a=2, b=1
    cache.get("a")                 # freq a=3, b=1
    cache.put("c", 3)              # LFU -> evict 'b' (used least), NOT 'a'
    assert "a" in cache and "c" in cache
    assert "b" not in cache


def test_policy_divergence_explicit():
    """A trace where LRU and LFU pick DIFFERENT victims."""
    # Make 'a' frequently used but then let 'b' be the most RECENT.
    lru: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2, policy=LRUEviction())
    lfu: LRUCache[str, int] = LRUCache(capacity=2, policy=LFUEviction())
    for cache in (lru, lfu):
        cache.put("a", 1)
        cache.get("a")             # 'a' used twice total (insert + get)
        cache.put("b", 2)          # 'b' is now most-recent, used once
        cache.put("c", 3)          # over capacity
    # LRU evicts 'a' (least RECENT); LFU evicts 'b' (least FREQUENT).
    assert "a" not in lru and "a" in lfu
    assert "b" in lru and "b" not in lfu


# ── synchronized (single global lock) basic correctness ──
def test_synchronized_cache_behaves_like_core():
    cache: SynchronizedLRUCache[str, int] = SynchronizedLRUCache(capacity=2)
    cache.put("a", 1)
    cache.put("b", 2)
    cache.get("a")
    cache.put("c", 3)
    assert "b" not in cache
    assert cache.get("a") == 1 and cache.get("c") == 3


# ── segmented cache: capacity split sums to exactly `capacity` ──
def test_segmented_capacity_invariant_when_filled():
    cache: SegmentedLRUCache[int, int] = SegmentedLRUCache(capacity=64, segments=8)
    for i in range(500):           # far more than capacity
        cache.put(i, i)
    assert len(cache) <= 64
    assert cache.capacity == 64


def test_segmented_clamps_segments_to_capacity():
    cache: SegmentedLRUCache[int, int] = SegmentedLRUCache(capacity=4, segments=16)
    assert cache.segment_count == 4          # never more segments than capacity
    cache.put(1, 1)
    assert cache.get(1) == 1


# ── STRICT concurrency proof: Barrier => all threads race at one instant ──
def test_no_corruption_when_all_keys_fit():
    """16 threads write disjoint keys into a cache big enough to hold them all.

    With nothing evicted, EVERY put must read back its exact value and the final
    size must equal the number of distinct keys — proving no lost or torn nodes.
    """
    n_threads = 16
    per_thread = 500
    total = n_threads * per_thread
    # WHY: a segmented cache splits capacity across segments, so a generous global
    #      headroom (4x) guarantees NO segment overflows under uneven key hashing —
    #      only then does "all keys fit, nothing evicted" actually hold.
    cache: SegmentedLRUCache[str, str] = SegmentedLRUCache(capacity=total * 4, segments=16)
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)

    def writer(t: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()             # all 16 threads start hammering at one instant
        for i in range(per_thread):
            key = f"t{t}-k{i}"
            cache.put(key, f"{key}=v")

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=writer, args=(t,)) for t in range(n_threads)]
    for th in threads:
        th.start()
    for th in threads:
        th.join()

    assert len(cache) == total                       # nothing lost
    assert len(cache) <= cache.capacity              # invariant holds
    for t in range(n_threads):
        for i in range(per_thread):
            key = f"t{t}-k{i}"
            assert cache.get(key) == f"{key}=v"      # every value reads back intact


def test_capacity_invariant_holds_under_overflow_race():
    """Many threads overflow a SMALL cache on a shared keyspace.

    The cache may evict, but it must (a) never exceed capacity and (b) never return
    a torn/garbage value — any present key must read back its one deterministic value.
    """
    capacity = 64
    keyspace = 256
    n_threads = 24
    cache: SegmentedLRUCache[int, str] = SegmentedLRUCache(capacity=capacity, segments=8)
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)

    def hammer(_t: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        for k in range(keyspace):
            cache.put(k, f"val-{k}")               # deterministic value per key
            assert len(cache) <= capacity          # invariant checked mid-flight

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=hammer, args=(t,)) for t in range(n_threads)]
    for th in threads:
        th.start()
    for th in threads:
        th.join()

    assert len(cache) <= capacity
    present = 0
    for k in range(keyspace):
        v = cache.get_or_none(k)
        if v is not None:
            assert v == f"val-{k}"                 # present keys are never torn
            present += 1
    assert present == len(cache)                   # size matches what is actually readable


def test_concurrent_get_and_put_mixed():
    """Readers and writers race on the same striped cache; no exceptions, no corruption."""
    cache: SegmentedLRUCache[int, int] = SegmentedLRUCache(capacity=1000, segments=16)
    for k in range(500):
        cache.put(k, k * 10)
    n_threads = 20
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
    errors: list[str] = []

    def worker(t: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        for k in range(500):
            try:
                if (t + k) % 2 == 0:
                    cache.put(k, k * 10)
                else:
                    v = cache.get_or_none(k)
                    if v is not None and v != k * 10:
                        errors.append(f"torn read at {k}: {v}")
            except Exception as exc:               # any exception is a corruption signal
                errors.append(f"{type(exc).__name__}: {exc}")

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(t,)) for t in range(n_threads)]
    for th in threads:
        th.start()
    for th in threads:
        th.join()

    assert errors == []
    assert len(cache) <= cache.capacity

content.text:
All tests pass. Here's the complete, interview-ready design.

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

This is the first thing to lay out — a minute-by-minute guide so you're never lost about what comes next.

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate "LRU cache, O(1) get/put, evict when full, thread-safe", then propose scope cuts. Don't write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — state the 5 public calls (`get`, `get_or_none`, `put`, `remove`, `__len__`) with their signatures and what they raise. "Before I design classes, here's the contract external code calls." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — only the 5 boxes that matter: `LRUCache`, `Node`, `DoublyLinkedList`, `EvictionPolicy`, and the `SegmentedLRUCache` wrapper. Mention the concretes (`LRUEviction`, `LFUEviction`) in one sentence. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — Strategy (eviction) + Template Method (the `_lock` hook), then immediately: "There's a check-then-act race in `put` — I'll fix it with lock striping at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 key insight** — "HashMap finds the node, doubly-linked list reorders it — that's how both get AND put are O(1). Eviction is just popping the tail." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (follow §6.0): exceptions → `Node` → `DoublyLinkedList` → `EvictionPolicy` + `LRUEviction` → `LRUCache` skeleton → `get` → `put`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 2 + Case 5** — walk the LRU eviction order and the LFU swap verbally, pointing at the code. Run `main.py` if it's a coding environment. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — describe the global-lock baseline, then lock striping. Write `SegmentedLRUCache` only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-ups** — use the hardest follow-ups from §9 (distributed cache, 10M keys, TTL). | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes — pick one at the top:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output. Demo the eviction order verbally with a state table. Write code but don't execute.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. Show the actual terminal output. The Barrier test is your proof of thread-safety.

**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** Right after drawing the diagram, proactively say "I see a check-then-act race in `put` — under two threads both can grow the map past capacity or corrupt the linked list." Said early and confidently, this signals you've built real concurrent systems.

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

A cache is a small, fast box of key→value pairs that sits in front of something slow (a database, a remote service). The catch is that it has a fixed size — when it fills up, we have to throw something out to make room. "LRU" means the thing we throw out is whoever hasn't been touched in the longest time, on the bet that recently-used stuff will be used again soon. The whole game is making `get` and `put` both O(1) *and* keeping it correct when many threads hammer it at once.

> 💬 "So this is the classic LRU cache: fixed capacity, O(1) get and put, and when it's full a `put` evicts the least-recently-used entry. The twist is thread-safety — lots of threads reading and writing concurrently. I'll build the clean single-threaded version first, then make it concurrent."

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (me proposing, interviewer confirming)

**You:** "I'll assume the core operations are `get`, `put`, and `remove`, with `put` doing both insert and update. Eviction happens automatically inside `put` when we're over capacity — the caller never triggers it explicitly. That the full surface?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's it."
*✓ scoped: eviction is a side-effect of `put`, not a public method.*

**You:** "For a miss, I'll offer two flavors: a strict `get` that raises `KeyNotFoundError`, and a `get_or_none` that returns `None`. Strict-by-default forces callers to handle misses; the `_or_none` is the ergonomic escape hatch. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "I like that — keep both."
*✓ the miss path is explicit, never a silent null that loses information.*

**You:** "Capacity must be a positive integer — I'll reject zero or negative with a typed `InvalidCapacityError` at construction, before any state exists. Agreed?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed."
*✓ validate at the boundary; a cache with no room is meaningless.*

**You:** "The prompt says eviction should be pluggable — LRU default, LFU as a harder cousin. I'll make eviction a Strategy so the cache doesn't hardcode the policy, but I'll keep the canonical O(1) LRU path front-and-center. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Good — show me both policies."
*✓ Strategy seam confirmed; LRU is the star, LFU proves the seam bends.*

**You:** "For thread-safety, I'll assume a single multi-core process with many threads — not multiple machines. So in-process locks, not a distributed lock. I'll mention the distributed story in follow-ups. Fair?"
**Interviewer:** "Fair — keep it in-process, but I'll ask about distributed later."
*if they push, the answer is a DB compare-and-set / Redis — flagged for §9.*

**You:** "And I'll prefer lock striping over one global lock — partition keys into segments, each with its own lock, so independent keys don't serialize. That's the ConcurrentHashMap model. Want me to go straight there?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes, that's exactly the interesting part."
*✓ lock striping is the §9 centerpiece — global lock is the baseline I compare against.*

**You:** "Last one — TTL / time-based expiry: out of scope for v1? It's a separate concern from recency eviction."
**Interviewer:** "Out of scope, good call."
*✓ cut TTL; if they want it, it's an extra timestamp + a sweep — flagged for §9 twists.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Get a value by key in O(1)** and mark it most-recently-used.
   - Hit → return the value, move the entry to the front of the recency order.
   - Miss → `get` raises `KeyNotFoundError`; `get_or_none` returns `None`.
2. **Put a key/value in O(1)**; insert if new, update in place if it exists.
   - New key over capacity → evict one entry (the policy's victim) before/after insert so size never exceeds capacity.
   - Existing key → overwrite the value, mark most-recently-used, size unchanged.
3. **Remove a key.**
   - Present → unlink it from both the map and the recency list.
   - Absent → raise `KeyNotFoundError`.
4. **Pluggable eviction policy.** LRU (default) evicts the least-recently-used; LFU evicts the least-frequently-used. Swapping is a new class, zero cache changes.
5. **Reject a non-positive capacity** at construction with `InvalidCapacityError`.
6. **Thread-safe under heavy concurrent get/put**, preferring lock striping (segmentation) over a single global lock for throughput.

**Out of scope**
- Time-based expiry / TTL (a separate timestamp + sweep job — not recency eviction).
- Distributed / multi-process caching (in-process threads only; distributed story in §9 follow-ups).
- Persistence / write-back to a backing store (we're the cache, not the source of truth).
- Cache statistics (hit rate, metrics) — trivial counters, not interesting here.

> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything: the one decision that drives everything is the data structure — I need O(1) for *both* get and put, which rules out a plain list or a single dict. I'll pair a HashMap with a doubly-linked list. With that locked, let me walk the actors and flows."

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **Application thread (caller)** — the only actor. It calls `get`/`put`/`remove`. There are *many* of these running concurrently; that concurrency is the whole challenge.
- **Eviction policy** — a system actor invoked by the cache when it's over capacity; it answers "who gets thrown out?".

There is no human, no background sweeper (we cut TTL), no external service. The cache is a pure in-memory data structure.

**Core flows**
1. Hit: caller `get(k)` → map lookup O(1) → move node to front → return value.
2. Miss: caller `get(k)` → map lookup returns nothing → raise `KeyNotFoundError`.
3. Insert + evict: caller `put(k,v)` on a full cache → create node, push front → over capacity → policy picks victim → unlink victim from map + list.
4. Update: caller `put(k,v)` on an existing key → overwrite value → move node to front → size unchanged.
5. Concurrent stampede: many threads `put`/`get` different keys → each key routes to its segment → only same-segment callers ever wait.

## 3. Core Entities (accept / reject)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough

> **Cache / LRUCache** — The thing the prompt asks for: owns capacity, the lookup map, the recency order, and coordinates eviction. It has both state and behavior, and every operation flows through it. *Accepted — this is the orchestrator.*
>
> **Node** — One entry. It's tempting to store values directly in a dict (`dict[key] = value`) and skip a node class. But the recency list needs a linked-list cell with `prev`/`next` pointers, and that cell must also remember its own `key` so eviction can delete the right map entry in O(1). That's real structural state. *Accepted — `Node` carries key, value, prev, next.*
>
> **DoublyLinkedList** — Could the cache just manipulate raw `prev`/`next` pointers inline? It could, but the pointer-surgery (push-front, move-to-front, pop-back with sentinels) is exactly the error-prone part, and it's reusable. Extracting it keeps the orchestrator readable and the O(1) guarantees auditable in one place. *Accepted — owns the recency order with sentinel head/tail.*
>
> **EvictionPolicy** — "Which entry to evict" is a pure policy decision the prompt explicitly wants pluggable (LRU vs LFU). That's the textbook signal for a Strategy: behavior that varies independently of the thing that uses it. *Accepted as an interface, with `LRUEviction` and `LFUEviction` concretes.*
>
> **Key / Value** — These are just the generic types `K` and `V` flowing through the API. They have no behavior of their own. *Rejected as entities — they're type parameters, not classes.*
>
> **Capacity** — A single integer invariant ("size ≤ capacity"). *Rejected — it's a field on the cache, validated at construction, not an entity.*
>
> **Segment / Lock** — In the thread-safe version each segment is a self-contained mini-cache with its own lock. The segment isn't a new *domain* concept though — it's literally a `SynchronizedLRUCache` reused. *Accepted as a structural part of `SegmentedLRUCache`, but it's the same cache class, not a new entity.*
>
> **Frequency count** — LFU needs to count accesses per entry. *Rejected as an entity — it's a `dict[Node, int]` living privately inside `LFUEviction`, not a first-class object.*

### 3.2 Final entity table

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `LRUCache` | Coordinate lookup, recency, and eviction | `_capacity: int`, `_map: dict[K, Node]` (id→node, O(1) find), `_order: DoublyLinkedList` (recency), `_policy: EvictionPolicy` (the seam) | `len(_map) <= _capacity` always; `_map` and `_order` hold exactly the same nodes |
| `Node` | Be one entry, linkable in the recency list | `key: K`, `value: V`, `prev: Node \| None`, `next: Node \| None` | Carries its own key so eviction is O(1) |
| `DoublyLinkedList` | Maintain recency order in O(1) | `_head: Node` (sentinel), `_tail: Node` (sentinel) | Front = MRU, back = LRU; real nodes always between the two sentinels |
| `EvictionPolicy` | Decide the victim when over capacity | *(interface — no state)* | `select_victim` never mutates the list |
| `LRUEviction` | Victim = tail of recency list | *(stateless)* | O(1) victim selection |
| `LFUEviction` | Victim = least-frequently-used | `_freq: dict[Node, int]` | Frequency count stays in sync with live nodes |

**Enums:** none. The states here are structural (a node's position in the list), not a labeled finite set — so no enum earns its place. (Contrast a parking-lot `SpotStatus`; recency has no discrete states, just an order.)

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro

> "So the model is small but dense. `LRUCache` is the brain — it owns the HashMap for O(1) lookup and the doubly-linked list for O(1) recency. `Node` is the clever bit: it lives in *both* structures at once and stores its own key, so when I evict the tail I know exactly which map entry to delete — no scan. `DoublyLinkedList` hides the sentinel-based pointer surgery so the O(1) moves are correct by construction. `EvictionPolicy` is the seam — `LRUEviction` is basically free because the list already orders by recency, and `LFUEviction` is the harder cousin that keeps its own counts. What I deliberately left out: Key and Value are just generics, Capacity is a validated field, and the frequency map is private to LFU — none of those earn entity status."

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

Once the entities exist, I pin down the public surface — the small set of methods callers actually touch — before designing internals. This is the contract §4–§7 implement.

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `get` | `(key: K) -> V` | the stored value | `KeyNotFoundError` | Read a key and mark it most-recently-used (Req 1) |
| `get_or_none` | `(key: K) -> V \| None` | the value, or `None` on a miss | — | Same read, but a miss is a normal answer, not an error (Req 1) |
| `put` | `(key: K, value: V) -> None` | — | — | Insert or update; evict the policy's victim if over capacity (Req 2) |
| `remove` | `(key: K) -> None` | — | `KeyNotFoundError` | Explicitly delete a key (Req 3) |
| `__len__` | `() -> int` | current entry count | — | How full the cache is; pairs with `capacity`/`is_full` (Req 5/6) |
| `is_full` | `() -> bool` | whether size == capacity | — | Cheap fullness check for callers (Req 5) |

Construction carries the rest of the contract: `LRUCache(capacity: int, policy: EvictionPolicy | None = None)` raises `InvalidCapacityError` on a non-positive capacity (Req 5), and `policy` defaults to `LRUEviction()` (Req 4). The thread-safe `SegmentedLRUCache(capacity, segments=16, policy_factory=...)` exposes the *same* `get/get_or_none/put/remove/__len__/is_full` surface — concurrency is invisible to the caller.

> 🎙️ "The whole cache is five calls: two reads (`get`, `get_or_none`), one write (`put`) that quietly handles eviction, one delete, and size introspection. Eviction is never a public method — it's a side-effect of `put`. If you handed me this interface cold, you'd know exactly what the cache does without seeing a single class, and you'd notice the thread-safe version has the *identical* surface — that's the point."

## 4. Class Design (derive each class, then the diagram)

### 4.1 Per-class derivation

#### 4.1.1 `LRUCache` (the orchestrator)

**(a) Role.** The public API and the brain. It owns the lookup map, the recency list, and the policy, and it coordinates the one non-trivial dance: lookup → reorder → (maybe) evict.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "Get a value by key in O(1)" | A map: `key → Node` |
| "Mark it most-recently-used" / "evict the least-recently-used" | The recency order: a `DoublyLinkedList` |
| "Pluggable eviction policy" | A reference to an `EvictionPolicy` |
| "Reject a non-positive capacity" | The `capacity` int (validated in `__init__`) |

```
class LRUCache:
    - _capacity: int
    - _map: dict[K, Node]
    - _order: DoublyLinkedList
    - _policy: EvictionPolicy
```

*State placement trade-off:* recency could live as a timestamp *on each Node* (relational state) or as *position in a shared list* (the orchestrator's structural state). A per-node timestamp makes "find the oldest" an O(n) scan; a shared ordered list makes it O(1) at the tail. Recency is a *relationship across all entries*, so it belongs in the orchestrator's list, not on the entity — that's why `_order` lives here.

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "Get a value by key in O(1), mark MRU" | `get(key) -> V` / `get_or_none(key) -> V \| None` |
| "Put a key/value, evict if over capacity" | `put(key, value) -> None` |
| "Remove a key" | `remove(key) -> None` |
| "Size ≤ capacity" introspection | `__len__() -> int`, `is_full() -> bool`, `capacity` |
| internal: drop one victim | `_evict_one() -> None` (private) |
| seam for thread-safety | `_lock(key)` (no-op hook) |

```
class LRUCache:
    - _capacity: int
    - _map: dict[K, Node]
    - _order: DoublyLinkedList
    - _policy: EvictionPolicy

    + LRUCache(capacity, policy=None)
    + get(key) -> V
    + get_or_none(key) -> V | None
    + put(key, value) -> None
    + remove(key) -> None
    + __len__() -> int
    + is_full() -> bool
    + capacity -> int                  (property)
    + __contains__(key) -> bool
    # _lock(key) -> contextmanager      (no-op; subclass overrides)
    - _evict_one() -> None
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why does `put` return `None`, not the evicted entry?** The caller asked to store something; the eviction is bookkeeping they didn't request. Returning the victim would leak an internal `Node` and tempt callers to depend on eviction order. If they need eviction events, that's an Observer hook (§9 twist).
- **Why a `_lock(key)` hook that does nothing?** It marks every critical section in the clean core *without* importing `threading`. The thread-safe subclass overrides *only* this method — the diff between "clean" and "locked" is one method, not the business logic. (Template Method.)
- **Why delegate the victim to a policy instead of just popping the tail inline?** Popping the tail *is* the LRU answer, but hardcoding it welds the cache to one policy. The seam costs one method call and buys LFU (and anything else) for free.

#### 4.1.2 `Node`

**(a) Role.** One cache entry, simultaneously a dict value and a linked-list cell.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "Get a value by key" | `value` (and `key`, see below) |
| "Recency order is a linked list" | `prev`, `next` pointers |
| "Evict in O(1)" | `key` — so the tail node knows which map entry to delete |

```
class Node:
    - key: K
    - value: V
    - prev: Node | None
    - next: Node | None
```

**(c) Operations derivation.** A `Node` is a pure data cell — all pointer surgery lives in `DoublyLinkedList`, so `Node` has no methods beyond construction.

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| hold key/value/links | `Node(key, value)` (fields only) |

```
class Node:
    - key: K
    - value: V
    - prev: Node | None
    - next: Node | None

    + Node(key, value)
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why store `key` on the node when the map already has it?** Because eviction starts from the *node* (the list tail), not the key. Without the back-reference, "which key owns this node?" is an O(n) scan — the single most common LRU bug. The `key` field makes `del _map[victim.key]` O(1).
- **Why `__slots__`?** A cache can hold millions of nodes; `__slots__` drops the per-instance `__dict__` and shrinks memory measurably. Cheap win, stated as a production-vs-interview nicety.

#### 4.1.3 `DoublyLinkedList`

**(a) Role.** The recency order, with all O(1) pointer surgery hidden behind named operations.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "Front = MRU, back = LRU" | A `_head` sentinel and a `_tail` sentinel |

```
class DoublyLinkedList:
    - _head: Node    (sentinel, never holds data)
    - _tail: Node    (sentinel, never holds data)
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| insert a new MRU entry | `push_front(node)` |
| mark an entry MRU on access | `move_to_front(node)` |
| unlink an entry (remove/evict) | `remove(node)` |
| find the LRU victim | `back() -> Node` |
| LFU tie-break by recency | `iter_back_to_front()` |
| emptiness guard | `is_empty() -> bool` |

```
class DoublyLinkedList:
    - _head: Node
    - _tail: Node

    + DoublyLinkedList()
    + push_front(node) -> None
    + move_to_front(node) -> None
    + remove(node) -> None
    + back() -> Node
    + is_empty() -> bool
    + iter_back_to_front() -> Iterator[Node]
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why two sentinel nodes?** With a permanent dummy head and tail, *every* real node always has a non-null `prev` and `next`. That deletes the "is this the first/last node?" branches from `remove` and `push_front` — the pointer code becomes four unconditional assignments, which is where O(1) correctness comes from.
- **Why `move_to_front = remove + push_front`?** Reusing the two primitives makes the reorder obviously correct instead of hand-rolling a third pointer dance.

#### 4.1.4 `EvictionPolicy` (Strategy interface)

**(a) Role.** The seam that answers "who gets evicted?" — and is told about accesses so stateful policies (LFU) can keep counts.

**(b) State derivation.** The interface is stateless; concretes carry their own state (LFU's frequency map).

```
interface EvictionPolicy:
    (no state — abstract)
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this interface |
|---|---|
| notice an access (get / update) | `record_access(node)` |
| notice a new entry | `record_insert(node)` |
| notice a departure | `record_removal(node)` |
| pick the victim | `select_victim(order) -> Node` |

```
interface EvictionPolicy:
    + record_access(node) -> None
    + record_insert(node) -> None
    + record_removal(node) -> None
    + select_victim(order: DoublyLinkedList) -> Node
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why four methods when LRU only needs one?** Because LFU needs the other three to maintain counts. LRU implements them as no-ops (recency already lives in the list). The asymmetry — LRU ignores the hooks, LFU uses them — is exactly what the seam is for.
- **Why pass the `DoublyLinkedList` into `select_victim` instead of giving the policy its own structure?** So LRU stays a one-liner (`return order.back()`) and LFU can break frequency ties by recency without duplicating the list.

#### 4.1.5 `LRUEviction` & `LFUEviction` (Strategy concretes)

**(a) Role.** The two shipped policies: LRU (default, free) and LFU (the harder cousin).

**(b) State derivation.** LRU is stateless; LFU keeps a frequency map.

```
class LRUEviction:        (stateless)

class LFUEviction:
    - _freq: dict[Node, int]
```

**(c) Operations derivation.** Both realize the four interface methods; LRU's hooks are no-ops.

```
class LRUEviction(EvictionPolicy):
    + record_access(node) -> None      (no-op)
    + record_insert(node) -> None      (no-op)
    + record_removal(node) -> None     (no-op)
    + select_victim(order) -> Node     (return order.back())

class LFUEviction(EvictionPolicy):
    - _freq: dict[Node, int]
    + LFUEviction()
    + record_access(node) -> None      (_freq[node] += 1)
    + record_insert(node) -> None      (_freq[node] = 1)
    + record_removal(node) -> None     (_freq.pop(node, None))
    + select_victim(order) -> Node     (min freq, ties by recency)
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why is LRU's `select_victim` O(1) but LFU's O(n)?** LRU rides the list's tail. LFU must find the minimum frequency, which is a scan unless you add frequency buckets — I note the O(1) upgrade in §5.5-C but keep the scan for interview clarity.
- **Why break LFU ties by recency?** Among equally-cold entries, evicting the least-recently-used is the standard refinement and prevents a tie from being resolved arbitrarily.

#### 4.1.6 `SynchronizedLRUCache` & `SegmentedLRUCache` (thread-safety)

**(a) Role.** Two ways to make the core thread-safe: `SynchronizedLRUCache` = one global lock (simple, serial); `SegmentedLRUCache` = lock striping (the throughput win).

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "thread-safe with one lock" | `SynchronizedLRUCache._global_lock: RLock` |
| "lock striping / segmentation" | `SegmentedLRUCache._segments: list[SynchronizedLRUCache]` |

```
class SynchronizedLRUCache(LRUCache):
    - _global_lock: RLock

class SegmentedLRUCache:
    - _capacity: int
    - _segments: list[SynchronizedLRUCache]
```

**(c) Operations derivation.** `SynchronizedLRUCache` overrides exactly one method; `SegmentedLRUCache` re-exposes the public surface and routes by hash.

```
class SynchronizedLRUCache(LRUCache):
    - _global_lock: RLock
    + SynchronizedLRUCache(capacity, policy=None)
    # _lock(key) -> contextmanager      (with self._global_lock: yield)

class SegmentedLRUCache:
    - _capacity: int
    - _segments: list[SynchronizedLRUCache]
    + SegmentedLRUCache(capacity, segments=16, policy_factory=None)
    + get(key) -> V
    + get_or_none(key) -> V | None
    + put(key, value) -> None
    + remove(key) -> None
    + __len__() -> int
    + is_full() -> bool
    + segment_count -> int              (property)
    - _segment_for(key) -> SynchronizedLRUCache
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why does `SegmentedLRUCache` *compose* `SynchronizedLRUCache` instead of subclassing `LRUCache` and striping locks over one shared map?** Because a single shared linked list guarded by per-key locks would still corrupt — two threads in different "stripes" would both splice the *same* list. True striping requires the *data* to be partitioned too. So each segment is a complete, independently-locked mini-cache. A key's hash picks its segment; different segments never touch each other's list.
- **Why split capacity across segments?** So the per-segment caps sum to exactly `capacity`, keeping `len(cache) <= capacity` exact. The trade-off: eviction is per-segment, so a hot segment can evict while another has room — the same approximation Guava and ConcurrentHashMap accept for scalability.

**Relationships, one at a time:**

| Class / Interface | Pattern | One-line reason |

| `SegmentedLRUCache` | Composite / lock striping | composes S `SynchronizedLRUCache` segments behind one façade |
| `Node` + `DoublyLinkedList` + `_map` | (intrusive) doubly-linked hash map | the structural idea that makes get *and* put O(1) |

> 🎙️ **Box-by-box:** "I put `LRUCache` in the center — every call flows through it. Hanging off it is `DoublyLinkedList`, which it *owns* — that's the recency order, MRU at the front, LRU at the back. The `Node` objects are shared between that list and the `_map` dict — same objects, two access paths, and that sharing is what makes both operations O(1). On the right is `EvictionPolicy`, the Strategy seam — the cache asks it 'who's the victim?' and never knows whether it's talking to LRU or LFU. `LRUEviction` is a one-liner; `LFUEviction` carries its own frequency map. Below, `SynchronizedLRUCache` *is* an `LRUCache` that overrides just the `_lock` hook, and `SegmentedLRUCache` wraps S of those for lock striping. The key seam is that dashed arrow to `EvictionPolicy`: new eviction rules plug in as new classes, zero core changes."

> ⚠️ **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG:** "I want to flag the race before you ask. `put` is a check-then-act: it reads `len(_map)`, decides whether to evict, and splices the linked list — three steps that are *not* atomic. Two threads calling `put` at once can both see room and both grow the map past capacity, or worse, interleave their `push_front` pointer writes and corrupt the list into a cycle or a dropped node. `get` is also a mutation because it does `move_to_front`. I'll keep the core clean and single-threaded for now, and in §9 I'll show the fix: a global lock as the simple baseline, then lock striping — S segments each with its own lock — as the throughput win, exactly like Java's ConcurrentHashMap."

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)

I drew the class boundaries by asking "who has the information to do this job?" `LRUCache` owns allocation and the lookup map because it's the only thing that sees *all* entries and the capacity. `DoublyLinkedList` owns the pointer surgery because it's the only thing that holds the sentinels and the invariant "front = MRU". `Node` owns its own `key` because eviction starts from the node and only the node can cheaply name its map entry. `LFUEviction` owns the frequency map because frequency is *its* concern, not the cache's — the cache shouldn't know LFU counts anything. Each piece holds exactly the state its decisions need, and nothing else.

### 5.2 Applied patterns

**Strategy — `EvictionPolicy`.**
- **Why it fits here:** "which entry to evict" is *precisely* the thing the business will want to tweak — LRU today, LFU tomorrow, maybe "LRU but pin these keys" next quarter. Extracting `EvictionPolicy` means the cache's `_evict_one` is three lines (`select_victim`, unlink, notify) and the *rule* lives in a separate class.
- **Interface + impls:** `EvictionPolicy.select_victim(order) -> Node` → `LRUEviction` (return tail), `LFUEviction` (min frequency).
- **🎙️ Script:** "Eviction policy is a Strategy. The cache asks 'who's the victim?' and the policy answers. Swapping LRU for LFU is constructing a different policy object — the cache never changes."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "The cache doesn't decide who to kick out — it asks a rule-of-the-day. Swap the rule, the cache doesn't notice."

**Template Method — the `_lock` hook.**
- **Why it fits here:** I want the clean core to have *zero* lock code so the data-structure design is the star, but I also want thread-safety to be a tiny, obvious diff. A no-op `_lock(key)` context manager in the base, overridden in the subclass, does exactly that.
- **Interface + impls:** `LRUCache._lock` (yields nothing) → `SynchronizedLRUCache._lock` (`with self._global_lock: yield`).
- **🎙️ Script:** "Every critical section in the core is wrapped in `with self._lock(key)`. In the base that's a no-op. The thread-safe subclass overrides that one method — the business logic never changes."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "I left a labeled placeholder where a lock *would* go. The safe version drops a real lock into the placeholder; nothing else moves."

### 5.3 SOLID (tied to named classes)
- **Single Responsibility:** `Node` holds data, `DoublyLinkedList` holds order, `EvictionPolicy` holds the eviction rule, `LRUCache` coordinates. Four reasons to change, four classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new eviction rule (LFU, MRU, segmented-LRU) is a new `EvictionPolicy` subclass — zero edits to `LRUCache`.
- **Liskov:** `SynchronizedLRUCache` is a drop-in `LRUCache` — same surface, stronger guarantee. Any code holding an `LRUCache` works unchanged with the synchronized one.
- **Dependency Inversion:** `LRUCache` depends on the `EvictionPolicy` abstraction, never on `LRUEviction` directly — the default is injected in the constructor.

### 5.4 Earn your patterns
Every pattern above survives `Cmd-F`: `EvictionPolicy` is a real ABC with two real implementations the cache calls through; `_lock` is a real context manager really overridden in `SynchronizedLRUCache`. I do **not** claim Singleton (there's no enforced single instance — you can make many caches), and I do **not** claim Factory for `Node(...)` (that's plain construction, not type translation). The one thing I'd label an **extension point (not wired in v1)** is an eviction-event Observer — the seam is obvious (`_evict_one` is where you'd fire it) but I didn't build it because nothing needs it yet.

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

**Insight:** Pair a HashMap (key→node) with a doubly-linked list (recency order), and let each node live in *both* at once. The map gives O(1) "where is this key"; the list gives O(1) "reorder by recency" and O(1) "who's the coldest". Eviction is then literally "pop the tail and delete its key from the map."

**Why it works:** The two operations a cache needs — *find by key* and *order by recency* — are served by two different ideal structures. A dict is O(1) for find but has no order; a linked list is O(1) for reorder but O(n) for find. Neither alone gives O(1) for both. But because the node stored in the dict *is* the same node linked in the list, you can jump from "found by key" to "its position in the recency order" in one pointer hop — so you get both O(1)s simultaneously. Lock striping then layers on top: because independent keys live in independent segments, concurrency doesn't serialize the whole cache.

**What breaks without it:** The naive version stores values in a plain dict and tracks recency with a Python list of keys (`recency.remove(key); recency.append(key)`). `list.remove` is O(n) — so every `get` is O(n), and at 100K entries under load the cache becomes the bottleneck it was supposed to remove. The subtler bug is the one that bites in production: people store recency as a *timestamp on each value* and "find the oldest" with a scan — also O(n), and now eviction is a linear sweep on every `put` once full. The linked-list-plus-map design makes both paths O(1) by construction.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm

**`get(key)` — the lookup-and-promote read.**

```
1. node = map.get(key)
2. if node is None: raise KeyNotFoundError   (or return None for get_or_none)
3. order.move_to_front(node)        # this access is now the most recent
4. policy.record_access(node)       # LRU: no-op; LFU: bump frequency
5. return node.value
```

> 🎙️ **(lookup method)** "This is an O(1) hit on the `_map` dict — that's the index I built so reads never scan. Step 3 is the LRU heartbeat: a read isn't read-only, it *promotes* the entry to the front. Step 4 is where the policy fires — for LRU it does nothing because the list already tracks recency, but for LFU it's the frequency bump. The edge an interviewer probes — 'is `get` really O(1) if it moves a node?' — yes: `move_to_front` is two unlink-relink hops, no traversal."

**`put(key, value)` — the insert-update-evict pipeline.**

```
1. existing = map.get(key)
2. if existing is not None:                  # UPDATE path
3.     existing.value = value
4.     order.move_to_front(existing)
5.     policy.record_access(existing)
6.     return
7. node = Node(key, value)                   # INSERT path
8. map[key] = node
9. order.push_front(node)
10. policy.record_insert(node)
11. if len(map) > capacity:
12.     evict_one()                           # select victim, unlink, delete map entry
```

> 🎙️ **(pipeline method)** "This is the pipeline — two phases. First the update fork (steps 2-6): same key just overwrites the value and re-promotes, and critically size doesn't grow. Then the insert fork (7-12): new node goes to the front, into the map, and *only then*, if we're over capacity, we evict. Step 11 is the check-then-act I flagged — `len(map) > capacity` read separately from the splice in steps 8-9 is the race. Step 12 is where Strategy pays off: `evict_one` asks the policy for the victim instead of hardcoding 'pop the tail'."

**`remove(key)` — explicit delete.**

```
1. node = map.pop(key, default=None)
2. if node is None: raise KeyNotFoundError
3. order.remove(node)
4. policy.record_removal(node)
```

> 🎙️ **(redemption/teardown method)** "Remove is one lookup and one unlink. `map.pop` does the find-and-delete in a single O(1) step; then we unlink from the list and tell the policy to drop its bookkeeping. The `record_removal` is safe to call even if the policy never tracked the node — LFU's `pop(node, None)` no-ops — so eviction and explicit remove can't collide."

**`_evict_one()` — drop exactly one victim.**

```
1. victim = policy.select_victim(order)   # LRU: order.back(); LFU: min frequency
2. order.remove(victim)
3. del map[victim.key]                     # O(1) thanks to victim carrying its key
4. policy.record_removal(victim)
```

**(3) DRY RUN — `put` on a full cache (capacity = 2, LRU policy):**

Initial state: `map = {a→Na, b→Nb}`, `order` front→back = `[b, a]` (b is MRU, a is LRU), so a `get("b")` happened recently.

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `existing = map.get("c")` | `None` — "c" is new |
| 7 | `node = Node("c", 3)` | new node Nc created |
| 8 | `map["c"] = Nc` | `map = {a→Na, b→Nb, c→Nc}` |
| 9 | `order.push_front(Nc)` | order front→back = `[c, b, a]` |
| 10 | `policy.record_insert(Nc)` | LRU no-op |
| 11 | `len(map)=3 > capacity=2` | over capacity → evict |
| 12a | `victim = policy.select_victim(order)` | `order.back()` → `Na` (a is LRU) |
| 12b | `order.remove(Na)` | order front→back = `[c, b]` |
| 12c | `del map["a"]` | `map = {b→Nb, c→Nc}` |
| 12d | `policy.record_removal(Na)` | LRU no-op |

Final: `map = {b→Nb, c→Nc}`, `order = [c, b]`, size = 2 = capacity. "a" — the least-recently-used — is gone, exactly as intended.

> *Edge probe: what if a second thread calls `put("d", 4)` between step 8 and step 11?* *Both threads could read `len(map)` after both inserts, see 4 > 2, and each evict one — but they might pick the same victim or splice the list concurrently and corrupt it. That's the race; §9 wraps steps 7-12 in the key's segment lock so the whole pipeline is atomic per segment.*

### C. Data Structure Choices

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `dict[K, Node]` | key→node lookup | natural O(1) find; a list of (key,node) would be O(n) per get — fatal for a cache | O(1) get/put |
| `DoublyLinkedList` (sentinels) | recency order | O(1) move-to-front and pop-tail; a Python `list` makes `move_to_front` an O(n) `remove`+`append`; a singly-linked list can't unlink an arbitrary node in O(1) (no `prev`) | O(1) all ops |
| `Node` with `key` back-ref | O(1) eviction | without the key on the node, evicting the tail needs an O(n) "which key is this?" scan | O(1) evict |
| `LFUEviction._freq: dict[Node,int]` | frequency counts | dict bump is O(1); `select_victim` scans for the min — O(n). I'd switch to **frequency buckets** (a dict `freq→ordered set of nodes` + a `min_freq` pointer) past ~10K entries to make LFU eviction O(1), at the cost of more bookkeeping | O(1) access, O(n) victim |
| `list[SynchronizedLRUCache]` segments | lock striping | S independent locks let unrelated keys proceed in parallel; one global lock serializes everything | O(1) route |

I deliberately chose the **O(n) LFU scan over frequency buckets** for the interview: at the scale of a single cache instance (thousands of entries) the scan is invisible, and the bucket structure triples the state to keep in sync (forget to move a node between buckets on access → wrong victim). The flip threshold I'd state out loud: "frequency buckets once a segment crosses ~10K entries and LFU eviction shows up in a profile."

### D. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix

> **🧠 Claim:** Each `Node` must store its own `key`, not just its value.
> **🔬 Proof:** Eviction starts from the linked-list *tail* (a `Node`), but the entry must also be deleted from the `_map`, which is keyed by `key`. If the node doesn't know its key, `del _map[???]` forces a scan of the whole map to find which key points at this node — O(n) per eviction, defeating the entire design.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Node.__init__` stores `key`; `_evict_one` does `del self._map[victim.key]` in O(1). The single most common LRU bug, designed out.

> **🧠 Claim:** A `get` is a *mutation*, not a read.
> **🔬 Proof:** `get` calls `move_to_front`, which rewrites four `prev`/`next` pointers. Two concurrent `get`s on the same node can interleave those writes and splice the node out of the list (lost entry) or into a self-loop (infinite `iter_back_to_front`).
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `get` runs inside `with self._lock(key)`. In the core that's a no-op; in `SynchronizedLRUCache`/`SegmentedLRUCache` it's a real lock, so the four pointer writes are atomic.

> **🧠 Claim:** `record_removal` must be safe to call on a node the policy never saw.
> **🔬 Proof:** Eviction and an explicit `remove` of the same key can both reach `record_removal` for the same node in a race; the second call must not blow up.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `LFUEviction.record_removal` is `self._freq.pop(node, None)` — a missing node is a silent no-op. LRU's is a no-op entirely.

> **🧠 Claim:** Sentinel head/tail nodes are what make `remove` branch-free.
> **🔬 Proof:** Without sentinels, removing the first node needs `if node.prev is None: head = node.next` and removing the last needs a symmetric tail fix — two branches that are easy to get wrong and a frequent source of NPEs.
> **🔬 Fix in code:** `DoublyLinkedList` seeds permanent `_head`/`_tail` sentinels in `__init__`, so every real node has non-null neighbours and `remove` is four unconditional assignments.

> **🧠 Claim:** Splitting capacity across segments must sum to *exactly* `capacity`.
> **🔬 Proof:** If each of 16 segments naively got `capacity // 16`, a capacity of 100 would yield 16×6 = 96 — the cache would silently hold only 96, violating the contract.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `SegmentedLRUCache` uses `divmod(capacity, segments)` and hands the remainder to the first few segments, so the caps sum to exactly `capacity` and `len(cache) <= capacity` is exact.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> def get(self, key):
>     value = self._store[key]            # plain dict of key -> value
>     self._recency.remove(key)           # _recency is a list[key]
>     self._recency.append(key)           # move to "most recent" (end)
>     return value
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** `list.remove(key)` scans the list to find the key — O(n). So `get` is O(n), not O(1), and eviction (`self._recency.pop(0)`) plus the matching `del self._store[oldest]` is another O(n) hidden in `put`. At a few hundred entries it looks fine in a demo and falls over the moment it's under real load. The correct version keys a dict by node and threads those same nodes through a doubly-linked list, so promote and evict are pointer hops, not scans.
