## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** I'm showing `put` on a full cache — the insert-then-evict path — because it touches *every* class (the map, the list, and the Strategy policy) and it's where the check-then-act race lives.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Caller
    participant Cache as LRUCache
    participant Map as _map dict
    participant List as DoublyLinkedList
    participant Policy as LRUEviction

    Caller->>Cache: put("c", 3)
    Cache->>Map: get("c")
    Map-->>Cache: None (new key)
    Cache->>List: push_front(node_c)
    Cache->>Policy: record_insert(node_c)
    Cache->>Cache: over capacity ?
    Cache->>Policy: select_victim(order)
    Policy->>List: back()
    List-->>Policy: node_b (the LRU)
    Policy-->>Cache: node_b
    Cache->>List: remove(node_b)
    Cache->>Map: del map["b"]
    Cache->>Policy: record_removal(node_b)
    Cache-->>Caller: None (done)
```

**🎙️ Walk it arrow by arrow:** "The caller calls `put("c", 3)` on `LRUCache`. First the cache asks the map 'do you have c?' — the map says `None`, so this is an insert. The cache pushes a new node to the front of the list and tells the policy `record_insert` (a no-op for LRU). Now the interesting moment: the cache checks `len(map) > capacity` — we're over — so it asks the *policy* `select_victim`. This is where Strategy fires: the policy doesn't make the cache guess; it calls `back()` on the list, gets `node_b` (the least-recently-used), and hands it back. The cache then does the atomic teardown — `remove(node_b)` from the list, `del map["b"]` from the map (O(1) because `node_b` carries its own key), and `record_removal` so the policy drops any bookkeeping. Then it returns. Notice the cache never decided *who* to evict — it only decided *when*."

**Why the sequence shows the design is good:** `LRUCache` coordinates the flow but implements no eviction *policy* — every "who" decision delegates to `select_victim`. Swap LRU for LFU and this exact sequence is unchanged except `back()` becomes a frequency scan. That's Open/Closed at runtime.

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**DEFAULT: describe the solution. Write code only if the interviewer asks.**

### The race, verbally

🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race.** "The problem is in `put` and `get` — and `get` is the surprising one because a read mutates the list via `move_to_front`. Take two threads calling `put` on a near-full cache. Both read `len(map)`, both see room (or both see 'over capacity'), and both run `push_front`, whose four pointer writes interleave. The list ends up corrupt — a node pointing at itself, or a node dropped while the map still references it. This is a check-then-act problem: the check (`len > capacity`) and the act (splice + evict) aren't one atomic step."

🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution.** "Wrap the critical section in a lock. Before `put` touches the map or list, it acquires the lock; it releases after the splice-and-evict completes. Now the two threads race for the *lock*, not the list — one wins and finishes a clean insert+evict, the other waits and then runs against consistent state."

🗣️ **Step 3 — Name the trade-off.** "One global lock is correct but serial — only one thread is ever inside the cache, even for unrelated keys. The throughput fix is **lock striping**: split the keyspace into S segments, each a complete mini-cache with its own lock. A key always hashes to the same segment, so a thread on key 'x' and a thread on key 'y' in different segments never wait on each other. Same idea as Java's ConcurrentHashMap and Guava's cache."

🗣️ **Step 4 — The distributed case (if asked).** "Across machines an in-process lock is useless — each process has its own memory. I'd push atomicity to a shared store: Redis (which is single-threaded, so its `GET`/`SET`/`LRU` are already atomic) or a DB compare-and-set. The recency/eviction then lives in Redis's own `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru`, and my app just reads/writes."

### Concurrency Vocabulary

| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
|---|---|---|
| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once; the result depends on timing | `push_front` + `len(map)` check in `put` |
| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the whole `with self._lock(key)` body |
| **Check-then-act** | You check a condition, then act — but state changed in between | "saw room, then inserted" |
| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing; no thread observes a half-done state | what the segment lock gives the put pipeline |
| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` per segment |
| **Reentrant (RLock)** | The SAME thread can re-acquire a lock it already holds | guards against a guarded method calling another |
| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers — coarse (global) vs fine (per-segment) | we chose per-segment, not one global |
| **Lock striping / segmentation** | Partition the keyspace into N independent shards, each with its OWN lock, so unrelated keys never contend | `SegmentedLRUCache`: S `SynchronizedLRUCache` segments, key → segment by hash |
| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock BEFORE touching state (assume conflict) | the in-process segment `RLock` |
| **Optimistic locking** | Don't lock; write conditionally and retry if you lost | the DB compare-and-set for the distributed case |
| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | Atomic "update only if the value is still X" | `UPDATE ... WHERE version = ?` distributed eviction |
| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs — both wait forever | avoided: we hold ONE lock at a time |

> The candidate sentence to drop: "It's a check-then-act race in `put`'s critical section; I take a pessimistic per-segment `RLock` — reentrant so a guarded method can call another — and I get throughput from lock striping. For distributed I'd switch to optimistic compare-and-set."

### Concurrency Follow-up Q&A

> ❓ "Why lock striping instead of one global lock?"
> "One global lock serializes the entire cache — every get and put waits on the same token, even for completely unrelated keys, so you get zero parallelism on a 32-core box. Striping gives each segment its own lock, so throughput scales with the number of segments. The cost is that eviction is per-segment, not global — a hot segment can evict while another has room — but that approximation is exactly what ConcurrentHashMap and Guava accept, and it's invisible at the hit-rate level."

> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
> "A plain `Lock` deadlocks if the same thread tries to acquire it twice. In the current code no guarded method calls another, so a `Lock` would work — but I use `RLock` defensively: the moment someone refactors `put` to call a guarded helper (say a guarded `_touch`), a plain `Lock` self-deadlocks and an `RLock` just re-enters. It's cheap insurance for a common refactor."

> ❓ "Why not a read-write lock — the cache is read-heavy?"
> "Tempting, because gets vastly outnumber puts. But `get` *mutates* — it calls `move_to_front` to update recency — so it's not a pure reader. A read-write lock only helps when readers don't write, and here every read writes the list. I'd only reach for an RW-lock if I made reads not reorder (e.g. an approximate-LRU 'clock' algorithm that just sets a bit), which is itself a great scaling answer."

> ❓ "How does the segment count interact with capacity?"
> "I split capacity across segments so the per-segment caps sum to exactly the total — `divmod(capacity, segments)` with the remainder spread over the first few — and I clamp segments to at most capacity so no segment rounds down to zero room. More segments means less contention but coarser per-segment LRU; 16 is the usual sweet spot, matching old ConcurrentHashMap's default concurrency level."

> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
> "None here. A deadlock needs two locks acquired in different orders by different threads. Every operation touches exactly one segment lock and never holds two at once — `get`, `put`, `remove` all route to a single segment. The only multi-segment operation is `__len__`, and it takes each segment lock independently and releases it before the next, so there's no hold-and-wait cycle."

> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-critical-section?"
> "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so there's no orphaned lock to clean up — the half-done state dies with it. In the distributed Redis/DB version, the atomic CAS either committed or didn't; there's no partial state and no lock to release, which is one more reason CAS beats a distributed mutex."

### If asked: clean → thread-safe (the actual code)

**Part A — the no-op hook + the subclass.** The core's `_lock` does nothing; the synchronized subclass overrides only it.

```python
# ─── §6 core: the hook does nothing ───────────────────────────────────────
@contextmanager
def _lock(self, key: K) -> Iterator[None]:
    yield                                  # no locks anywhere in the clean core

# ─── §9: one global RLock (the simple, serial baseline) ────────────────────
class SynchronizedLRUCache(LRUCache[K, V]):
    def __init__(self, capacity: int, policy: EvictionPolicy | None = None) -> None:
        super().__init__(capacity, policy)
        self._global_lock = threading.RLock()

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, key: K) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._global_lock:            # ignore the key — one lock for the whole cache
            yield
```

Then lock striping composes S of those, each with its own lock:

```python
# ─── §9: lock striping — the throughput win ────────────────────────────────
class SegmentedLRUCache(Generic[K, V]):
    def __init__(self, capacity, segments=16, policy_factory=None) -> None:
        if capacity < 1:
            raise InvalidCapacityError(f"capacity must be >= 1, got {capacity}")
        if segments < 1:
            raise InvalidCapacityError(f"segments must be >= 1, got {segments}")
        segments = min(segments, capacity)             # never round a segment down to 0
        make_policy = policy_factory or LRUEviction
        self._capacity = capacity
        base, remainder = divmod(capacity, segments)   # caps SUM to exactly capacity
        self._segments = [
            SynchronizedLRUCache(base + (1 if i < remainder else 0), make_policy())
            for i in range(segments)
        ]

    def _segment_for(self, key):
        return self._segments[abs(hash(key)) % len(self._segments)]

    def get(self, key):           return self._segment_for(key).get(key)
    def get_or_none(self, key):   return self._segment_for(key).get_or_none(key)
    def put(self, key, value):    self._segment_for(key).put(key, value)
    def remove(self, key):        self._segment_for(key).remove(key)
    def __contains__(self, key):  return key in self._segment_for(key)
    def __len__(self):            return sum(len(seg) for seg in self._segments)
```

Three notes the interviewer scores on: (1) `RLock` not `Lock` (reentrant, defensive against guarded-calls-guarded); (2) **per-segment** locks, not one global, so unrelated keys run in parallel; (3) the data is partitioned too — each segment owns its *own* map and list, because striping locks over one shared list would still corrupt it.

**Part B — the exact `put`, clean vs thread-safe.** The body is *identical*; only `_lock` changes underneath it (Template Method), so there's literally no diff in the method itself — the lock comes from the subclass.

```python
# ─── §6 version (clean) — _lock yields nothing ─────────────────────────────
def put(self, key: K, value: V) -> None:
    with self._lock(key):                          # no-op in the core
        existing = self._map.get(key)
        if existing is not None:
            existing.value = value
            self._order.move_to_front(existing)
            self._policy.record_access(existing)
            return
        node: Node[K, V] = Node(key, value)
        self._map[key] = node
        self._order.push_front(node)
        self._policy.record_insert(node)
        if len(self._map) > self._capacity:
            self._evict_one()

# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — SAME body; _lock now takes the segment lock ──
# Inherited unchanged from LRUCache. SegmentedLRUCache routes put("x", …) to the
# segment whose SynchronizedLRUCache._lock("x") wraps `with self._global_lock`.
# The check-then-act (len(map) read + push_front + evict) is now ATOMIC per segment.
```

🎙️ "The diff from clean to thread-safe is *zero lines in `put`*. That's the whole point of the Template-Method hook — the business logic is written once, and thread-safety is injected by overriding `_lock`. The `with self._lock(key)` that was a no-op in the core becomes a real per-segment lock in the subclass, so the read-decide-splice-evict sequence runs atomically."

**Part C — prove it with a Barrier test.** (Shown and run green in §7.2.) 16 threads, Barrier-synced to collide at one instant, write 8000 disjoint keys into a striped cache with headroom → `len == 8000`, every value reads back intact, `len <= capacity` throughout. A second test overflows a 64-slot cache from 24 threads and asserts the size bound holds *mid-flight* and no present key is ever torn.

🎙️ "The Barrier makes all 16 threads hit the striped locks at the same instant — without it they'd take turns and there'd be no real race. Every key survives with the right value and the size bound never breaks. That's the proof."

### Edge cases handled

- Get a missing key → `KeyNotFoundError` → covered by `test_get_missing_raises_key_not_found`.
- Remove a missing key → `KeyNotFoundError` → covered by `test_remove_missing_raises_key_not_found`.
- Non-positive capacity → `InvalidCapacityError` at construction → covered by `test_non_positive_capacity_rejected`.
- Non-positive segment count → `InvalidCapacityError` → covered by `test_segmented_rejects_bad_segment_count`.
- Update an existing key → value overwritten, size unchanged → covered by `test_update_existing_key_does_not_grow_size`.
- Store a value of literally `None` → distinguishable from absent (check the node, not the value) → covered by `test_value_none_is_stored_distinctly_from_missing`.
- Capacity 1 → insert-then-evict still works (insert makes size 2, evict drops to 1) → exercised by the overflow tests.
- More segments than capacity → segments clamped to capacity, no zero-room segment → covered by `test_segmented_clamps_segments_to_capacity`.
- Concurrent overflow → `len(cache) <= capacity` always, no torn values → covered by `test_capacity_invariant_holds_under_overflow_race`.

### Extensibility

**(i) Level calibration.** How many of these you'll be asked tracks your target level: a junior may get none, a mid-level one or two, a senior several with depth. I'll walk three twists fully; the patterns in §5 are what make each a small diff.

**(ii) Summary table (at-a-glance proof).**

| Want to add | How (one class / method) | What in the core changes |
|---|---|---|
| LFU eviction | `LFUEviction(EvictionPolicy)` (already shipped) | nothing — inject a different policy |
| MRU / FIFO / "pin these keys" | new `EvictionPolicy` subclass | nothing |
| TTL / time-based expiry | add `expires_at` to `Node` + a guard in `get` | one line in `get` (`if expired: raise/None`) |
| Eviction events (Observer) | fire a callback inside `_evict_one` | one line in `_evict_one` |
| Approximate-LRU at huge scale | swap `move_to_front` for a "set a bit" clock policy | one method (`record_access`) |

Every row is "nothing" or "one line" — that's the Strategy + Template-Method seams paying off.

**(iii) Twist walkthroughs.**

> **Twist 1 — "Add a time-to-live: entries expire after N seconds even if they're hot."**
>
> Recency eviction and time expiry are orthogonal — an entry can be the most-recently-used and still be stale. Right now nothing expires by wall-clock.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I'd add an `expires_at` timestamp to `Node`, set from an *injected* clock at `put`, and add one guard at the top of `get`: if the node is past its expiry, treat it as a miss and evict it lazily. A background sweep can also reclaim expired nodes proactively. The clock is injected so tests fast-forward instead of sleeping."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> # Node gains one field
> self.expires_at: float | None = None
>
> # get() gains one guard (clock injected as self._now)
> if node.expires_at is not None and self._now() >= node.expires_at:
>     self._order.remove(node); del self._map[key]; self._policy.record_removal(node)
>     raise KeyNotFoundError(f"key expired: {key!r}")
> ```
> **Trade-off:** lazy expiry is free on the hot path but lets dead entries occupy space until touched; add the sweep only if memory pressure from stale entries is real. Worth it for a session/token cache, overkill for a pure hot-set cache.

> **Twist 2 — "Make eviction observable — notify a listener whenever something is evicted."**
>
> Today eviction is silent; a caller that wants to write-back the victim or log it has no hook.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "This is a textbook Observer. I take an optional list of listeners in the constructor and fire them inside `_evict_one`, *after* the unlink so the cache is already consistent. The core stays one line heavier; listeners are new classes."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> def _evict_one(self) -> None:
>     victim = self._policy.select_victim(self._order)
>     self._order.remove(victim)
>     del self._map[victim.key]
>     self._policy.record_removal(victim)
>     for listener in self._on_evict:            # the one new line
>         listener(victim.key, victim.value)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** listeners run inside the (segment) lock, so a slow listener blocks that segment — for heavy work I'd hand the victim to a queue and notify outside the lock, exactly like keeping notifications out of the critical section.

> **Twist 3 — "Support a 'second-chance' policy: give a hot entry one reprieve before eviction."**
>
> This is the CLOCK approximation of LRU — cheaper than true LRU at scale because a `get` just sets a bit instead of relinking.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "It's a new `EvictionPolicy`. `record_access` sets a 'referenced' bit on the node instead of moving it; `select_victim` walks from the tail, and the first node whose bit is set gets its bit cleared and skipped (its 'second chance'), the first unset node is the victim. Zero cache changes — it's purely a policy swap."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class ClockEviction(EvictionPolicy):
>     def __init__(self): self._ref: dict[Node, bool] = {}
>     def record_access(self, node): self._ref[node] = True       # just set a bit
>     def record_insert(self, node): self._ref[node] = False
>     def record_removal(self, node): self._ref.pop(node, None)
>     def select_victim(self, order):
>         for node in order.iter_back_to_front():
>             if self._ref.get(node):  self._ref[node] = False     # second chance
>             else:                    return node
>         return order.back()
> ```
> **Trade-off:** approximate-LRU under read-heavy load lets reads avoid the relink (and a read-write lock becomes viable), at the cost of slightly worse hit rates than exact LRU. Worth it past the scale where `move_to_front` contention shows in a profile.

### Hardest follow-up questions (distinct from the twists)

> ❓ "What's the single hardest concurrency bug in this design, and how do you know the test catches it?"
> "Torn linked-list pointers: two `put`s interleaving their `push_front` writes so a node points at itself or is dropped while the map still holds it — and the size counter wouldn't even notice. I catch it with the Barrier test that writes 8000 disjoint keys concurrently and then asserts *every* value reads back and `len == 8000`. A torn list would either lose a key (count < 8000) or infinite-loop the readback. The per-segment lock makes the splice atomic, so it can't happen."

> ❓ "What if you have multiple app servers — how does the cache stay correct across machines?"
> "An in-process lock only guards one process, so I'd stop using a Python cache as the source of truth and move to a shared store. Redis is the standard answer: it's single-threaded so its operations are atomic, and `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru` gives me LRU eviction for free. My code becomes `GET`/`SET` calls; the recency and eviction live in Redis. If I needed app-side caching too, I'd layer a local cache per server and accept eventual consistency, invalidating via pub/sub."

> ❓ "What if the cache holds 10M entries — does anything break?"
> "The data structures stay O(1), so latency is fine, but two things bite. First, memory: 10M `Node`s — `__slots__` already trims that, and I'd consider off-heap or a slab allocator. Second, lock contention on a hot segment: I'd raise the segment count (more stripes = less contention) and switch LFU's `select_victim` to frequency buckets so eviction stays O(1). At that scale I'd also profile whether `move_to_front` on every read is the bottleneck and, if so, move to the CLOCK approximate-LRU from Twist 3 so reads only set a bit."

> ❓ "A `put` triggers a write-back to a slow database that fails halfway — how do you avoid a corrupt cache?"
> "Keep the cache mutation and the external write separate, and do the cache part first under the lock, the external part *outside* it. The cache update (map + list + evict) is all in-memory and can't 'fail halfway' — it either runs fully under the lock or not at all. The DB write-back happens after the lock releases; if it fails I retry or enqueue it, but the cache is already consistent. I never hold the segment lock across the slow I/O — that's the same 'notify outside the lock' rule, applied to write-back."

### 🎙️ 30-second summary

"The key insight is pairing a HashMap with a doubly-linked list and sharing the same node between them — that's what makes both `get` and `put` O(1), and eviction just pops the tail. Eviction policy is a Strategy, so LRU is a one-liner and LFU plugs in with zero cache changes. The one concurrency risk is a check-then-act race in `put` — and `get` too, since it reorders — which I guard with a Template-Method `_lock` hook: a global lock as the simple baseline, then lock striping into S independent segments as the throughput win, proved by a 16-thread Barrier test with no lost or torn nodes. For distributed scale I'd push atomicity to Redis or a DB compare-and-set."
